meta.description: Dwarf origin story — A craftmaster's apprentice whose forge was seized by a megacorp

dwf_001.title: The Last Forge
text: The smell of solder and ozone clings to your beard like a second skin. You stand four feet eleven inches in a world built for six-footers, but down here in the sub-level foundries of the Lower District, the ceilings hang low enough to make the tall ones stoop. Down here, for once, the world fits you.

Master Bronnik's forge occupies the back half of a repurposed water treatment plant. Three furnaces, two plasma cutters, a CNC lathe older than your grandmother, and enough raw stock to keep a dwarf happy for a lifetime. The air shimmers with heat. Sparks cascade like orange rain from the grinding wheel where your master shapes a neural housing with hands that haven't trembled in sixty years of work.

You've been Bronnik's apprentice for nine years. Nine years of burns, of twelve-hour shifts, of learning to read metal the way other races read faces. You can tell the carbon content of steel by the sound it makes when you tap it. You can feel stress fractures through your fingertips. This is what dwarves were made for, not the gene-labs or the corporate towers, but the forge. The honest heat. The weight of a hammer in your hand.
Bronnik doesn't look up from his work. 'The Harken order,' he says. 'Twelve servo-actuators, military grade. You finish the housing calibrations yet?'

choices.text: 'Finished them at dawn. Tolerances within half a micron.'
'Still working on the last three. The alloy's giving me trouble.'

dwf_002.title: Pride in the Work
text: Bronnik pauses the grinding wheel. The sudden silence fills the forge like a held breath. He picks up one of the housings from your workbench, turns it in his scarred fingers, holds it to the light. His left eye, replaced decades ago with a magnification implant that most surgeons couldn't afford, clicks and whirs as it focuses.
He sets it down without a word. But there's the faintest twitch at the corner of his mouth. For Bronnik, that's a standing ovation.

'Half a micron,' he repeats. 'Your grandmother could do a quarter, but she had forty years on you.' He returns to the grinding wheel. 'There's food in the back. Eat. Then start on the trigger assemblies. The client wants them by week's end.'

choices.text: Eat quickly and get to work on the trigger assemblies.
Ask Bronnik about the client. Military-grade work means questions.

dwf_003.title: The Stubborn Alloy
text: Bronnik kills the grinding wheel and walks over. He doesn't say anything. He doesn't need to. He picks up the housing you've been fighting with and rolls it between his palms like a piece of clay. His implant eye clicks, refocusing.

'You're heating too fast,' he says. 'This alloy has titanium traces, military surplus. It remembers stress. You have to bring it up slow, let it forget what it was before you can make it into something new.' He sets it down. 'Like people.'
He returns to his bench. 'Fix it. Then eat. You look like a smelted rat.'

choices.text: Take his advice. Reheat the alloy slowly, patiently.
Ask him about the alloy's origin. Military surplus is unusual.

dwf_004.title: Hands and Heat
text: You eat standing up because there's nowhere to sit that isn't covered in tools or stock. The food is the same as always: protein paste on flatbread, a thermos of something Bronnik calls coffee but which tastes like motor oil filtered through regret. It doesn't matter. Dwarves don't eat for pleasure. They eat to work.

The trigger assemblies are delicate work. Each one requires threading a filament of conductive wire through a housing no wider than a straw, then soldering it to contact points with a steady hand and a hotter-than-hell micro-torch. One tremor, one breath at the wrong moment, and the whole thing fuses into slag.

You finish the first one in forty minutes. The second takes thirty. By the fifth, your hands have found their rhythm and the world outside the circle of your work lamp ceases to exist. There is only the wire, the solder, the heat, and the thing taking shape beneath your fingers.
This is the closest a dwarf gets to prayer.

choices.text: Keep working through the night. Finish the whole batch.
Take a break. Step outside for air.

dwf_005.title: Questions About the Client
text: Bronnik's hands stop. That never happens. In nine years, you've seen earthquakes, power outages, and a fire that took his left eyebrow, and his hands never stopped.

'The client,' he says carefully, 'is a man named Harken. He pays in hard currency. He doesn't ask for receipts. And he doesn't answer questions.' He turns to look at you, his implant eye glowing amber in the forge light. 'That's all you need to know.'
But you've been around long enough to read the lines on his face. There's something else there, something you haven't seen before on the old dwarf. It takes you a moment to place it.
Fear.

choices.text: Drop it. If Bronnik doesn't want to talk, there's a reason.
Press him. 'Master, if this work is dangerous, I need to know.'

dwf_006.title: The Night Shift
text: Hours dissolve. The forge hums its low iron song. Bronnik left sometime around midnight, his heavy boots scraping the grated floor as he trudged to the cot in the back room. You barely noticed. The work has you now, and when the work has you, there's nothing else.

By four in the morning, all twelve trigger assemblies sit in a row on your bench like soldiers at attention. Perfect. Each one identical to the last within tolerances that would make a machine jealous. You lean back, crack your neck, and feel the satisfying ache in your shoulders that means you've done something real.
That's when you hear it. A sound that doesn't belong in the foundry at this hour. The front gate, the heavy blast-rated one that Bronnik installed after the last break-in attempt, is being cut open.

choices.text: Grab a hammer and move toward the sound.
Wake Bronnik. Now.

dwf_007.title: The Alley
text: The alley behind the forge smells like rust and standing water. You lean against the corrugated wall and breathe air that's only marginally better than the fumes inside. Above you, the underbelly of the next district blocks out whatever sky might exist. Pipes and conduits run overhead like mechanical intestines, dripping condensation that might be water or might be something worse.

A pair of dwarves from the forge two blocks down nod as they pass. Kettik and Volna, husband and wife, both machinists. They've been doing piecework for one of the mid-tier corps, stamping out identical chassis plates eight hundred times a day. Assembly line work. The kind that would make your grandfather spit.

Volna pauses. 'Heard Bronnik's got a big contract,' she says. 'Military?' You shrug. She lowers her voice. 'Be careful. Word in the tunnels is that Nexus-Krieg is buying up forge contracts in the district. The ones they can't buy, they take.'
She moves on before you can ask what she means.

choices.text: Go back inside and tell Bronnik what Volna said.
Head to the Sector 7 bar for a real drink and more information.

dwf_008.title: The Truth About Harken
text: Bronnik sits on an upturned crate and rubs his face with both hands. For the first time since you've known him, he looks old. Not dwarf-old, which is dignified and stone-like, but old in the way that means something inside has cracked.

'Harken works for Nexus-Krieg,' he says. 'Or he did. He's building something, a weapons system, off the books. He came to me because corp forges leave digital trails. Independent shops like ours don't.' He pauses. 'I took the job because we needed the money. The district levies went up again. Another month without a big contract and I'd have to sell the plasma cutters.'

He looks at the forge around him, at the walls stained with decades of honest work, the tools hanging in their proper places, the anvil that his father brought from the old country before the old country was paved over and turned into a server farm.
'This place is all I have,' he says. 'All we have. And I'd rather build guns for a ghost than watch it die.'

choices.text: 'Then we finish the job and keep our heads down.'
'We should find out what Harken is really building.'

dwf_009.title: The Hammer
text: Your fingers close around the handle of Bronnik's five-pound cross-peen hammer. It's not a weapon. It's a tool. But in a dwarf's hand, the distinction is academic.

The cutting sound intensifies. Whoever's on the other side of that gate has an industrial torch, the kind that can chew through blast-rated steel in minutes. Sparks spray inward, and a line of cherry-red metal traces the outline of a door-sized rectangle in the gate.

Three figures step through the hole before the edges have stopped glowing. They're wearing matte-black tactical gear with no insignia. Their helmets have opaque visors. They move like people who've been trained to enter buildings where they're not welcome.

The one in front sees you and raises a hand. Not a weapon. A hand. A gesture that says stop. 'Independent Forge 7-Sigma,' a modulated voice says through the helmet speaker. 'This facility is now under acquisition review by Nexus-Krieg Industrial. Stand down and comply.'

choices.text: Stand your ground. 'This forge belongs to Bronnik. You're trespassing.'
Back away slowly. Wake Bronnik. He needs to handle this.

dwf_010.title: Waking the Master
text: You hammer on the back room door. 'Master! Someone's cutting through the gate!'

Bronnik is up in seconds, faster than a dwarf his age has any right to move. He emerges with a forge-hammer in one hand and something you've never seen before in the other, a compact pistol, dwarven-made, with custom engravings along the barrel. He built it himself. Of course he did.

He pushes past you and stops at the end of the corridor. The cutting has stopped. The gate hangs open, its edges still glowing. Three figures in black tactical gear stand in the entrance to his forge. His forge. The place where his father worked, and his father's mother before that.

'Bronnik Ironhand,' the lead figure says. 'Nexus-Krieg Industrial is exercising eminent domain over this facility under Municipal Code 7-41. You have sixty seconds to collect personal effects and vacate.'
The old dwarf's face goes through every shade of red you've ever seen and several new ones. His grip on the hammer tightens until his knuckles go white.

choices.text: Stand beside Bronnik. Whatever happens, you face it together.
Try to reason with the intruders. There has to be a legal avenue.

dwf_011.title: Sector 7 Bar
text: The Sector 7 bar doesn't have a name. It doesn't need one. It's the only place in three blocks where you can get a drink that won't blind you, and even that's a close thing. The ceiling is low enough for you, which is rare and pleasant. Most of the clientele are dwarves and humans, with a few orcs hunching in the back booths.
You find a spot at the bar and order whatever's cheapest. The bartender, a one-armed dwarf named Gillis who lost the limb in a forge accident twenty years ago, slides you something brown and caustic.

'You're Bronnik's kid,' he says. Not a question. Everyone in the district knows everyone. 'Tell him Nexus-Krieg sent people to the Vostok forge yesterday. Sealed the place. Kicked out old Therren and his whole family. Thirty years of work, gone overnight.'

He leans closer. 'They're coming for every independent shop in the district. Some kind of acquisition sweep. The ones who cooperate get buyout offers that amount to pocket change. The ones who don't...' He draws a finger across his throat.

choices.text: Rush back to the forge and warn Bronnik.
Ask Gillis if anyone is organizing resistance.

dwf_012.title: Heads Down
text: You work through the night together in silence. Master and apprentice, side by side at the bench, doing what dwarves do. The trigger assemblies take shape under your combined hands. Bronnik hums an old forge-song under his breath, something in the old tongue that your grandmother used to sing. The melody is about stone and fire and things that last.

By dawn, the Harken order is complete. Twelve servo-actuators, twelve trigger assemblies, all packed in foam-lined cases and ready for pickup. Bronnik inspects each piece with his implant eye, turning them slowly, checking tolerances. He nods.
'Good work,' he says. Two words. From Bronnik, that's a sonnet.

The pickup is scheduled for noon. You have a few hours. Bronnik tells you to get some sleep, but neither of you moves. There's a feeling in the air, like the pressure drop before a storm, and dwarves have always had a sense for when the ground is about to shift.

choices.text: Rest your eyes at the workbench. Just for a minute.
Go check the front gate. Make sure the locks are solid.

dwf_013.title: Investigating Harken
text: 'You want to investigate a Nexus-Krieg ghost operative?' Bronnik stares at you like you've suggested they swim to the moon. 'You've got stone between your ears, just like your father.'

But he doesn't say no. Instead, he crosses to the far wall and pulls open a drawer you've never seen him open before. Inside, nested in oilcloth, is a datapad. Not the cheap consumer kind. This is military-spec, with encryption hardware soldered directly to the motherboard.

'Harken gave me the specs on this,' Bronnik says. 'The weapon system he's building. I never looked too closely. Didn't want to know.' He hands you the pad. 'But if you're determined to stick your beard into the gears, you might as well know what we're making.'

You power on the datapad. The schematics that load make your blood run cold. It's not a weapons system. It's a neural override array, a device designed to hijack the motor functions of anyone with a cranial implant. In a city where sixty percent of the population has some form of neural augmentation, this thing could turn thousands of people into puppets.

choices.text: 'We have to destroy this. All of it. The specs, the parts, everything.'
'We need to find out who Harken is planning to use this on.'

dwf_014.title: Dwarven Defiance
text: You plant your feet the way Bronnik taught you to stand at the anvil. Wide. Low. Immovable. The hammer hangs at your side, but your grip says everything your mouth doesn't need to.

'This forge has been in operation for three generations,' you say. 'Before Nexus-Krieg existed. Before this district had a name. You don't walk into a dwarf's forge and tell him to leave. Not today. Not ever.'
The lead operative tilts his helmeted head slightly. Amusement, maybe. Or recalculation. 'We have legal authority under Municipal—'

'I don't care what you have. You're standing in Bronnik Ironhand's forge with your boots on his floor and your breath on his air. You want this place? You're going to have to carry me out, and I promise you I'm heavier than I look.'
There's a pause. Then one of the operatives in the back reaches for something on his belt.

choices.text: Swing the hammer before he draws.
Hold your ground. Don't strike first.

dwf_015.title: Side by Side
text: You step up beside Bronnik. He glances at you, and for half a heartbeat something cracks in that granite face. Gratitude, maybe. Or sorrow. He hands you the pistol. 'Safety's on the left. Pull, don't squeeze. And for stone's sake, don't shoot my lathe.'
Together you face the three operatives. Master and apprentice. An old dwarf and a young one. Two hammers and a homemade pistol against tactical armor and corporate authority.

The lead operative shifts his weight. He wasn't expecting resistance. The dwarves in the other forges probably went quietly, probably signed whatever was put in front of them and shuffled off to find new lives in the scrap yards. But Bronnik didn't build this forge to hand it to someone in a helmet.
'Last warning,' the operative says. 'Vacate or face forcible removal and criminal charges.'
Bronnik smiles. It's not a pleasant smile. 'Lad,' he says to you without taking his eyes off the intruders, 'did I ever tell you about the time your grandmother threw a taxman through a wall?'

choices.text: 'Let's make her proud.' Raise the pistol.
'Master, we can't win a fight here. We need another way.'

dwf_016.title: The Legal Angle
text: 'Under what authority?' you ask, stepping forward with your hands visible. 'Independent forges in the Lower District are protected under the Artisan Preservation Act of—'

The lead operative cuts you off. 'The Artisan Preservation Act was repealed eighteen months ago under Corporate Consolidation Order 2041. All independent manufacturing operations in Sectors 5 through 9 are subject to acquisition review by authorized corporate entities.' He pulls a datapad from his vest and holds it up. 'This is the acquisition order. Signed by a district magistrate.'
You look at the document. The signature is real. The legal language is airtight. They've done this before, many times, and they've learned to close every loophole.
Bronnik's jaw works silently. He can read legal documents the way he reads metal: looking for the flaw, the weak point, the stress fracture. But this time there's nothing to find.

choices.text: 'Master, we need time. Ask them for the thirty-day appeal window.'
There's no legal way out. Look at Bronnik and nod toward the back door.

dwf_017.title: The Resistance
text: Gillis pours himself a drink with his one hand, a practiced motion that speaks of years. He checks the bar, making sure no one's listening too closely.

'There's a woman named Kessa. Half-elf, runs a repair shop near the transit hub. She's been talking to the forge masters, trying to organize something. A guild, a union, something with teeth.' He takes a drink. 'Most of the dwarves think she's crazy. But most of the dwarves haven't had their shops seized yet.'
He slides you a chip across the bar. 'Her address. Tell her Gillis sent you. And tell Bronnik to lock his damn gate tonight.'
You pocket the chip and finish your drink. The brown liquid burns all the way down, which is how you know it's working.

choices.text: Head to the transit hub to find Kessa now.
Go back to the forge first. Bronnik needs to know about this.

dwf_018.title: The Dream of Stone
text: You close your eyes and the forge disappears. In its place, you see mountains. Real mountains, not the concrete and steel kind that pass for geography in the megacity. These are the mountains from the old stories, the ones your grandmother told you about when you were small enough to sit in her lap. Mountains with veins of ore running through them like blood through a body. Mountains where dwarves carved halls that lasted ten thousand years.

In the dream, you're standing in one of those halls. The ceiling arches overhead, supported by pillars carved with the faces of ancestors you'll never know. A forge burns at the center, larger than anything you've ever seen, its heat a living thing that wraps around you like an embrace.
You reach for the hammer that waits on the anvil, and just as your fingers close around the handle—
The explosion knocks you off the bench.

choices.text: Scramble to your feet. What the hell was that?

dwf_019.title: The Gate
text: The front gate is three inches of blast-rated steel with a mag-lock that Bronnik designed himself. You check the seals, test the lock mechanism, run your fingers along the hinges. Everything's solid. Everything's tight.

But there's something on the ground outside the gate. You peer through the viewing slot and see it: a small device, no bigger than your fist, attached to the base of the gate with a magnetic clamp. A red light blinks on its surface in a steady, patient rhythm.
You know what it is. You've built enough demolition charges for Bronnik's mining clients to recognize the casing. Someone has attached a breaching charge to your front door.
The blinking accelerates.

choices.text: Rip the charge off the door and throw it down the corridor.
Run. Get away from the gate. Scream for Bronnik.

dwf_020.title: Destroying the Work
text: Bronnik stares at you. For a long moment you think he's going to argue, to talk about the money, the levies, the survival of the forge. Instead, he nods slowly.
'Your grandmother would have said the same thing,' he says. 'She always had more spine than sense.' He takes the datapad from your hands and places it on the anvil. Then he picks up his hammer.
One swing. The datapad shatters. The schematics, the specs, all of it reduced to fragments of glass and silicon. It feels good. It feels right.

Together you spend the next hour destroying every component of the Harken order. The housings go back into the furnace. The trigger assemblies are crushed under the hydraulic press. The servo-actuators are disassembled and their cores dissolved in acid. By the time you're done, there's nothing left but slag and satisfaction.
'Harken won't take this well,' Bronnik says, wiping his hands on his apron. 'And Nexus-Krieg will take it worse.'

choices.text: 'Then we prepare for what's coming.'
'We need to disappear. Tonight.'

dwf_021.title: Following the Thread
text: You scroll through the schematics on the datapad, looking for anything that might tell you who the target is. The technical specifications are dense, full of jargon that even your training can barely parse, but buried in the metadata you find something: a deployment radius calculation centered on a specific set of coordinates.

You pull up a map. The coordinates correspond to the Cloud Towers, the upper-city district where the megacorp executives live. Where the memory stacks are stored. Where the people who run this city sleep in clean air and sunlight.
'Stone and fire,' Bronnik whispers, looking over your shoulder. 'He's going to use it on the towers. On the executives themselves.'
A coup. Harken isn't just building a weapon. He's planning to seize control of the most powerful people in the city by hijacking their neural implants. And you've been building the components.

choices.text: 'This changes everything. We have leverage now.'
'We're in over our heads. Destroy it all and run.'

dwf_022.title: The Fight
text: You move first. Dwarves aren't fast, but in close quarters, in a space cluttered with workbenches and heavy machinery, speed matters less than knowing the terrain. You've worked this forge for nine years. You know every bolt in the floor, every hanging tool, every shadow.

The hammer catches the first operative in the knee joint of his tactical armor, where the plates don't overlap. He goes down with a sound like a bag of wrenches hitting concrete. The second one fires a taser, but you're already behind the lathe, and the prongs embed in three hundred pounds of cast iron.

Bronnik moves like a landslide. He doesn't fight like a young dwarf, all fury and momentum. He fights like a craftsman, precise, efficient, every motion calculated to achieve maximum result with minimum effort. His hammer takes the taser operative in the ribs. Something cracks.
The third one backs toward the gate. He's reaching for his comm unit. If he calls for backup, this is over.

choices.text: Throw the hammer. Hit the comm unit.
Let him call. You need witnesses to what Nexus-Krieg is doing.

dwf_023.title: Standing Stone
text: You don't swing. You stand. Feet planted, jaw set, eyes locked on the operative reaching for his weapon. There's an old dwarven saying: the mountain does not swing at the wind.
The operative draws a taser. Points it at your chest. The red targeting dot sits right over your heart. 'Last chance,' the lead one says.
You don't move. You don't blink.

The operative with the taser looks at his squad leader. There's a moment, a breath-long pause where the outcome teeters on a knife's edge. Then Bronnik steps up behind you, his hammer resting on his shoulder like a promise.

'You can shoot my apprentice,' the old dwarf says quietly. 'And then I'll kill all three of you with this hammer. Or you can walk out of my forge and tell your bosses that Bronnik Ironhand said no. Either way, I'm not leaving.'
The lead operative holds the silence for five seconds. Ten. Then he signals his team to lower weapons. 'This isn't over, Ironhand. We'll be back with enforcement drones. And a magistrate.'
They leave through the hole they cut in the gate.

choices.text: 'They'll be back. We need to prepare.'
'Master, we won this round. But we should talk about what happens next.'

dwf_024.title: The Back Door
text: Bronnik's jaw tightens. Every fiber of his dwarven stubbornness screams at him to stand and fight, to die in his forge if that's what it comes to. But he looks at you, and something shifts.

'The tunnels,' he says. 'Maintenance corridor behind the furnace. I dug it out twenty years ago, in case.' He grabs a bag from under his workbench, already packed. He's been expecting this. Maybe for weeks.

You grab what you can. Your tools, the ones your grandmother gave you, the ones that fit your hands like they were grown there. Bronnik takes his father's anvil stamp, a small iron seal no bigger than a coin, and tucks it inside his coat.

Behind the number-three furnace, hidden by a sheet of corrugated steel, there's a hole in the wall just wide enough for a dwarf. The maintenance corridor beyond is dark and smells like stagnant water.

Behind you, you hear the operatives beginning to inventory the forge. Your forge. And then you hear something worse: the sound of a hammer striking the anvil. But it's not Bronnik's rhythm. It's wrong. Everything about it is wrong.

choices.text: Keep moving. Don't look back.
Stop. Listen to Bronnik. He's not following.

dwf_025.title: The Appeal
text: The lead operative consults his datapad. 'Section 14-C of the Consolidation Order provides for a seventy-two-hour appeal window. Filed through the District Commerce Office.' He pauses. 'I should inform you that the Commerce Office processes an average of four hundred appeals per month. The approval rate is zero point three percent.'
Three days. You have three days to find a way to save the forge through a system designed to deny you.

Bronnik takes the appeal form the operative hands him. His fingers leave smudge marks on the datapad's screen, forge-grime that doesn't wash off because it's embedded in the whorls of his fingerprints. Written into his skin.
'Seventy-two hours,' he says, as the operatives file out through the hole they cut in the gate. 'That's barely enough time to sharpen a chisel.'
'It's enough time to find help,' you say.

choices.text: Head to the Sector 7 bar. Someone there might know a way.
Go to the transit hub. Find someone with legal connections.

dwf_026.title: The Transit Hub
text: The transit hub is a cathedral of noise and motion. Mag-rail trains scream through on elevated tracks, shaking the platform with each pass. Below, the concourse swarms with bodies, all of them moving, all of them going somewhere that isn't here.

You find the address on Gillis's chip. It leads to a repair shop wedged between a noodle stall and a shuttered pawn shop. The sign reads KESSA'S FIXES in hand-painted letters. Through the window you can see a half-elf woman bent over a disassembled servo motor, her silver hair tied back with a length of copper wire.
You knock. She looks up, sees a dwarf, and her expression goes through surprise, recognition, and calculation in about two seconds.
'Gillis sent you,' she says, not a question. 'Come in. Lock the door behind you.'

choices.text: Tell her everything. The forge, Nexus-Krieg, the acquisition sweep.
Be cautious. Ask what she knows before you reveal anything.

dwf_027.title: The Explosion
text: The blast rips through the front of the forge like a fist through paper. The gate, three inches of blast-rated steel that Bronnik swore could stop a tank round, peels inward like tinfoil. A wall of heat and pressure picks you up and throws you into the workbench. Tools scatter. Metal screams.

Your ears ring. The world is sideways. Through the smoke and dust you can see the forge, your forge, torn open like a wound. The front wall is gone. The furnaces are still running, their heat mixing with the cold night air in visible thermals. Somewhere, a pipe has burst and water sprays across the floor in a silver arc.
Bronnik. Where's Bronnik?

You find him behind the overturned anvil, blood on his face, his implant eye flickering. He's alive, but one of his legs is bent at an angle that makes your stomach turn. 'The bastards,' he whispers. 'They bombed my forge. They actually bombed my forge.'

choices.text: Get Bronnik out. Now. Before they come through the hole.
Check the perimeter. Are more coming?

dwf_028.title: Defusing the Situation
text: You know demolition charges. You've built enough of them. Your fingers find the magnetic clamp and twist it counterclockwise, forty-five degrees exactly, releasing the grip. The charge comes free in your hand, still blinking, still counting down to something terrible.

The corridor behind you runs thirty meters to a junction that opens onto the maintenance tunnels. You wind up like your grandmother taught you to throw a rivet hammer, from the hip, using your whole body, and hurl the charge down the corridor.

It detonates six seconds later. The blast rolls through the tunnel like thunder in a pipe, and a gout of flame and dust erupts from the corridor entrance. But the forge is intact. The gate is intact. Your hands are shaking, but everything that matters is still standing.
Bronnik appears behind you, hammer in hand, eyes wild. 'What in the name of every ancestor was that?'
'Someone tried to blow the gate,' you say. 'I threw their charge into the tunnels.'
He stares at you for a long moment. 'You threw a live breaching charge.' He pauses. 'Your grandmother would have liked you.'

choices.text: 'Someone wants this forge badly. We need to find out who.'
'We're not safe here anymore. We need to move.'

dwf_029.title: Fortifying the Forge
text: You spend the next hours turning the forge into a fortress. Dwarves have been fortifying positions since before humans discovered fire, and the instinct runs deep.

Bronnik welds scrap plate over the damaged gate while you rig tripwires from old detonator cord. You reinforce the corridor junctions with barricades made from engine blocks. You set up a warning system using the motion sensors from a scrapped security drone Bronnik keeps in the parts bin.

By the time you're done, the forge looks less like a workshop and more like a bunker. Bronnik surveys the work with a critical eye, his implant clicking as it measures angles and structural integrity.
'It'll hold against a squad,' he says. 'Maybe two. But if they bring drones or heavy armor...' He trails off. You both know the math.
A knock on the side door. Not a threatening knock. A careful one. Three taps, pause, two taps. The old dwarven signal for 'friend at the gate.'

choices.text: Open the door. That signal means it's someone from the district.
Check through the viewing slot first. Trust nothing tonight.

dwf_030.title: Running
text: The maintenance tunnels beneath the Lower District form a labyrinth that only dwarves, rats, and the truly desperate know how to navigate. You move through them with Bronnik, crouching under pipes, splashing through ankle-deep runoff, following a mental map built from years of maintaining the forge's plumbing.

Bronnik moves slowly. His leg, broken or badly strained during the explosion, drags behind him. He leans on your shoulder without comment. His weight is considerable. Dwarves are built like compressed stone.
Behind you, the forge grows smaller with every step. Everything you've ever known, every skill you've learned, every piece of yourself you've hammered into shape on that anvil, gets farther away.
'Where are we going?' you ask.

Bronnik is quiet for a long time. 'There's a woman at the transit hub,' he finally says. 'Name's Kessa. Half-elf. She's been trying to organize the independent forges.' He grunts with pain. 'I told her she was wasting her time. Guess I was the fool.'

choices.text: Head for the transit hub to find Kessa.
Find shelter first. Bronnik needs medical attention.

dwf_031.title: Leverage
text: 'Leverage?' Bronnik looks at you like you've lost your mind. 'Against Nexus-Krieg? You want to blackmail a corporation that has its own army?'

'Not blackmail. Insurance. If they know we have the schematics, they can't just disappear us. We become valuable alive.' You hold up the datapad. 'This is proof that one of their operatives is building a neural override weapon. If this gets out, their stock crashes, their board faces criminal charges, and every competitor in the city comes for blood.'

Bronnik sits with this for a moment, turning it over the way he turns metal, looking for the grain. 'You're thinking like a smuggler, not a craftsman,' he says finally. But there's a hint of respect in his voice.
'I'm thinking like a survivor,' you say.
'Same thing, in this city.' He stands. 'Alright. But we need copies. Multiple copies, hidden in different locations. And we need someone who knows how to make noise if we go quiet.'

choices.text: Make copies of the datapad and hide them around the district.
Find someone you trust to hold the information. Someone outside the district.

dwf_032.title: The Thrown Hammer
text: A dwarf's hammer throw is not a graceful thing. It's a violent, ugly, beautiful thing, all torque and rage and gravity channeled through a four-foot frame that was built to move mountains. The five-pound cross-peen leaves your hand like a comet.
It hits the comm unit dead center. The device explodes into fragments of plastic and circuitry. The operative staggers back, his hand bleeding, staring at the wreckage of his only lifeline.

Bronnik doesn't waste the opening. He hits the third operative once, in the helmet, with a blow that rings through the forge like a bell. The man drops. The forge goes quiet except for the hum of the furnaces and the sound of three unconscious bodies breathing.

'Well,' Bronnik says, lowering his hammer. 'That's assault on corporate security personnel. Three counts.' He looks at you. 'They'll send more. With guns. And drones. And probably a magistrate with a warrant.'
'Then we better not be here when they arrive.'

choices.text: Strip the operatives of useful gear and disappear into the tunnels.
Tie them up. You might need hostages or bargaining chips.

dwf_033.title: Witnesses
text: The operative makes the call. You hear the crackle of a response, a calm voice authorizing additional units. Bronnik gives you a look that says are you insane, but you shake your head.
'Neighbors,' you say. 'The forges on either side. Kettik, Volna, the others. They need to see this. They need to see what Nexus-Krieg does to dwarves who say no.'

You're right, and Bronnik knows it. By the time additional operatives arrive ten minutes later, six forge masters from the surrounding blocks are watching from their doorways. They see the cut gate. They see the tactical gear. They see Bronnik standing in his forge with blood on his face and a hammer in his hand.
The backup team, larger this time, four operatives and a drone, pauses at the gate. The lead one surveys the audience. Witnesses complicate things. Reports get filed. Footage gets leaked.
'Stand down,' the team leader orders his people. He points at Bronnik. 'This isn't over.' Then they withdraw, taking their fallen with them.

choices.text: Talk to the forge masters. It's time to organize.
This bought you time, not safety. Plan the next move with Bronnik.

dwf_034.title: The Hard Conversation
text: The forge is quiet. The kind of quiet that follows a storm, heavy and waiting. Bronnik sits on his anvil, which is something he never does because an anvil is a tool, not furniture, and tools deserve respect. The fact that he's sitting on it tells you more about his state of mind than any words could.

'I've been forging for sixty-one years,' he says. 'Started when I was six. My mother put a hammer in my hand and said, This is who you are. Make it true.' He turns the hammer over in his hands. 'I've survived three district riots, two corporate wars, and a plague that killed half the Lower District. But I always had the forge. The forge was the thing that didn't break.'

He looks at you, and for the first time you see the old dwarf not as your master, not as the granite-faced craftsman who can shape metal with his bare hands, but as a person standing at the edge of everything he knows.
'What do we do, lad? What do we do when they take the one thing that makes us who we are?'

choices.text: 'We build something new. That's what dwarves do.'
'We fight. We find allies. We take it back.'

dwf_035.title: Into the Dark
text: You push forward through the maintenance tunnels. The walls are damp concrete, cracked and stained with mineral deposits that gleam faintly in the light from emergency strips spaced too far apart. Water drips from overhead pipes in a rhythm that sounds like a clock counting down.
Behind you, the sounds of the forge fade. The clang of tools being tossed into crates. Voices, muffled and corporate. The sound of your life being inventoried by strangers.

Bronnik walks beside you in silence. His jaw is locked so tight you can see the muscles jumping in his cheeks. In one hand he carries the bag he packed. In the other, his father's anvil stamp. He hasn't let go of it since you left.
The tunnel forks. Left goes deeper, toward the scrap yards and the districts where nobody asks questions. Right goes up, toward the transit hub and the surface.

choices.text: Go left, deeper. Disappear completely.
Go right, toward the transit hub. You need allies, not hiding places.

dwf_036.title: Bronnik's Choice
text: You stop. Turn. Bronnik is standing at the tunnel entrance, half in shadow, half in the dying light of the forge. He's looking back at his life's work with an expression you've never seen on him before. Loss. Pure, uncut, bottomless loss.
'Go,' he says. 'Take the tools. Take what you can carry.'
'Master—'

'I said go.' His voice breaks on the word, just for a second, before the granite snaps back into place. 'I'm not leaving. This forge is mine. It was my father's. It'll be mine when they carry me out, and it'll still be mine when they burn my body in its furnace.' He turns to face the corridor, toward the sound of boots. 'A dwarf doesn't run from his anvil.'

You want to argue. You want to drag him into the tunnel by his beard. But you know that look. You've seen it on the faces of old dwarves in the stories, the ones who stood at the gates of mountain halls they knew would fall.
He grips his hammer and walks back toward the light.

choices.text: Honor his choice. Go. Carry what he taught you into the dark.
No. Turn back. You won't leave him.

dwf_037.title: Kessa's Network
text: You tell her everything. The forge, Bronnik, Nexus-Krieg's acquisition sweep, the Harken contract. Kessa listens without interrupting, her hands still working on the servo motor, her silver eyes tracking your words like data.

When you finish, she sets down her tools. 'Your forge makes twelve. Twelve independent shops seized or threatened in the last month. All in the lower sectors. All dwarven-owned.' She pulls up a holographic map from a projector built into her workbench. Red dots cluster in the Lower District like a rash. 'It's not random. They're targeting the forge district specifically.'
'Why?'

'Because dwarven forges are the only independent manufacturing in the city capable of producing military-grade components outside corporate oversight. As long as you exist, there's a supply chain they don't control.' She looks at you. 'You're not just craftspeople to them. You're a threat.'

She reaches under the workbench and pulls out a box. Inside are comm units, encrypted, hardened against surveillance. 'Take one. I'm building a network. When Nexus-Krieg comes for your forge, and they will, you won't face them alone.'

choices.text: 'I'm in. What do you need from me?'
'I need to get back to Bronnik. But I'll keep the comm.'

dwf_038.title: Testing Kessa
text: 'What do you know about Nexus-Krieg's operations in the forge district?' you ask, keeping your voice neutral.

Kessa doesn't blink. 'I know they've seized eleven forges in the last month. I know they're using a repealed preservation act as legal cover. I know they've got a magistrate in their pocket, and I know that the dwarves they've displaced are living in the scrap yards now, building nothing, making nothing, dying inside one day at a time.'
She meets your eyes. 'I also know that a dwarf who asks questions before giving answers is either very smart or very scared. Which are you?'
'Both,' you say.
'Good. The stupid brave ones are all dead already.'

choices.text: She's genuine. Tell her about Bronnik's situation.
Keep your cards close. Ask what she wants in return for help.

dwf_039.title: Carrying the Master
text: You get your shoulder under Bronnik's arm and lift. He's heavy, built like a barrel of iron filings, but dwarves are stronger than they look and terror is a hell of a stimulant. He grunts but doesn't cry out. Dwarves don't cry out.

Through the smoke you can see figures moving at the front of the forge. Not the tactical team from before. These are different, moving more carefully, with equipment. They're here to take inventory, not to fight. The explosion was just the key that opened the door.

You drag Bronnik toward the back corridor. His broken leg scrapes the floor and leaves a trail of blood on the grated walkway. His implant eye has stopped flickering and gone dark. He needs a medic, a real one, not the back-alley patch jobs that pass for healthcare in the Lower District.
'The tunnels,' he manages. 'Behind furnace three.'
You know. Of course you know. This is your forge too.

choices.text: Get him into the tunnels and head for the scrap yards.
Get him to the transit hub. Kessa's network might have medical contacts.

dwf_040.title: Scouting the Damage
text: You move toward the blast hole, keeping low, using the overturned workbenches as cover. Through the smoke and dust you can see the street outside. Two black vehicles, unmarked, idling in the rain. More operatives, six or seven, moving with purpose. One of them is carrying a box, the kind used to transport sensitive electronics. They're not here to destroy the forge. They're here to take it.
A drone hovers at rooftop level, its searchlight sweeping the interior through the blast hole. You duck behind the hydraulic press as the beam passes over you.

This isn't a raid. It's a surgical extraction. They bombed the gate to gain entry, and now they're going to strip the forge of everything valuable, the tools, the stock, the custom equipment that Bronnik spent a lifetime building.
You hear Bronnik coughing behind you. He's trying to stand, using his hammer as a crutch. Blood runs down his face from a gash above his ruined implant eye.

choices.text: You're outgunned. Get Bronnik and run.
There's a gas line running under the forge. You could blow the whole place.

dwf_041.title: Friends at the Gate
text: You open the side door. Four dwarves stand in the alley, rain dripping from their beards. Kettik and Volna from the forge two blocks down. Old Therren, who lost his shop to Nexus-Krieg last week. And a young dwarf you don't recognize, barely out of her apprenticeship by the look of her, carrying a toolbox that's almost as big as she is.
'We heard the noise,' Kettik says. 'Figured you'd need hands.' He looks past you at the fortified forge and nods approvingly. 'Good barricades. Amateur, but good.'

Therren pushes in without waiting for an invitation. He's thinner than you remember, and there's a haunted look in his eyes that wasn't there before they took his forge. 'They came for mine at night too,' he says. 'Three operatives. I went quietly.' He looks at Bronnik. 'I'm done going quietly.'
The young dwarf sets down her toolbox and cracks her knuckles. 'Name's Renna. I was Therren's apprentice. I can rig anything to explode, if that helps.'

choices.text: Welcome them in. Assign defensive positions.
This isn't their fight. Warn them about the risks.

dwf_042.title: Checking the Slot
text: You slide the viewing slot open. Outside, in the rain-slicked alley, you see dwarves. Four of them. Kettik and Volna, old Therren, and a young woman you don't know. They're unarmed except for tools, and they're soaking wet. Kettik catches your eye through the slot and holds up empty hands.

But there's something else. Behind them, at the end of the alley, you catch a glint of something. A lens. Someone is watching the side entrance of your forge with optics. Could be a drone operator. Could be a surveillance team.
You have visitors. You also have eyes on your door.

choices.text: Let the dwarves in but warn them they were followed.
Signal them to leave. It's too dangerous with watchers in the alley.

dwf_043.title: The Underground Road
text: The tunnels toward the transit hub are older than the district above them. Built during the first expansion, when the city was still pretending it had urban planning. Now they're a maze of abandoned infrastructure, home to those who've fallen through every crack the city has to offer.

You pass a camp of displaced people huddled around a chemical heater. They watch you with hollow eyes but don't speak. Further on, a makeshift market sells scrapped electronics and questionable food. A dwarf vendor recognizes Bronnik and offers water without asking for payment.
After an hour of walking, you see daylight, or what passes for it in the Lower District, filtering down through a grate above. The transit hub is close. You can hear the mag-rails screaming overhead.

Bronnik leans against the tunnel wall. If he's with you, his face is gray with pain. 'Kessa's shop is near the main concourse,' he says. 'She'll help. She has to help. She's been waiting for something like this.'

choices.text: Emerge at the transit hub and find Kessa's shop.
Rest here first. Gather your strength.

dwf_044.title: The Scrap Yard Medic
text: The scrap yards sprawl across the eastern edge of the Lower District like a metal graveyard. Mountains of discarded machinery, obsolete vehicles, and stripped electronics rise against the dark sky. Somewhere in this maze of rust and ruin, there are people who know how to put broken things back together, including broken dwarves.

You find the medic in a converted shipping container. She's human, middle-aged, with hands that are steadier than they have any right to be. Her name is Patch, because of course it is. She takes one look at Bronnik and shakes her head.
'Compound fracture. Tibia. And his ocular implant is fried.' She starts working without waiting for permission. 'This is going to hurt.'
'I'm a dwarf,' Bronnik says through gritted teeth. 'Everything hurts. Get on with it.'
Patch sets the bone with a portable osteo-welder. Bronnik doesn't scream, but his fingers leave dents in the metal table.

choices.text: While Patch works, explore the scrap yard for useful salvage.
Stay with Bronnik. He shouldn't be alone right now.

dwf_045.title: Copies in the Dark
text: You spend the night copying the datapad's contents onto everything you can find. Salvaged memory chips, burned onto optical discs, even transcribed by hand onto sheets of metal foil because a dwarf trusts engraving over electronics any day.

One copy goes into a sealed pipe behind the number-two furnace. One goes to Gillis at the bar, sealed in an envelope marked OPEN IF WE DISAPPEAR. One gets buried in the concrete floor of the maintenance tunnel, marked with a symbol only you and Bronnik would recognize.
By dawn, the information exists in six places. Nexus-Krieg would have to find them all. They'd have to know to look.
'Insurance,' Bronnik says, watching you seal the last copy. 'My grandmother called it spite. Same thing, I suppose.'

choices.text: Now contact Harken. Let him know the terms have changed.
Wait. Let Nexus-Krieg make the next move.

dwf_046.title: Spoils of War
text: You strip the unconscious operatives with efficient hands. Tactical vests, comm units, a taser with two charge packs remaining, three sets of tactical goggles with low-light capability, and a security keycard that might open doors you're not supposed to walk through.
Bronnik watches you work with something like approval. 'You're taking to this too naturally,' he says. 'Your mother would be horrified.'
'My mother would have taken their boots too.'

'Fair point. She was practical.' He packs what he can carry. The bag he'd prepared holds tools, the anvil stamp, a few personal items. Everything else stays. Everything else belongs to the forge, and the forge is no longer theirs.
You enter the maintenance tunnel together, leaving three unconscious operatives on the floor of a forge that three generations of Ironhands built with their bare hands.

choices.text: Head deep into the tunnels. Put distance between you and the forge.
Head for the transit hub. Bronnik mentioned someone named Kessa.

dwf_047.title: Prisoners
text: You bind the operatives with forge wire, the heavy-gauge stuff used for reinforcing molds. It'll take an industrial cutter to get through it. Bronnik removes their helmets. Three faces: two humans, one half-elf. Young. Corporate soldiers, the disposable kind.
The half-elf wakes first. His eyes go wide when he sees Bronnik standing over him with a hammer.

'Here's how this works,' the old dwarf says in a voice like grinding stone. 'You tell us who sent you, who authorized the acquisition, and how many other forges are on the list. In return, I don't use this hammer to test the structural integrity of your kneecaps.'
The operative swallows. 'Nexus-Krieg Acquisitions Division. Director Voss. There are... there are twenty forges on the list. Yours was number thirteen.'
'Lucky thirteen,' Bronnik mutters. 'And Director Voss, where do we find him?'
'The corporate tower. Level forty. But you'll never—'
'I didn't ask for your assessment of our chances.'

choices.text: Get more information. Press them about Nexus-Krieg's timeline.
You have what you need. Leave them tied up and disappear.

dwf_048.title: The Forge Masters' Council
text: They file into Bronnik's forge one by one. Kettik and Volna. Old Therren, already displaced, with nothing left to lose. Magda from the precision shop on the corner, who can cut glass with a plasma torch at fifty paces. Urist, the youngest forge master in the district, who inherited his shop when his mother died last year.
Six dwarves. Six forges. Sitting in a circle on upturned crates in a workshop that's one corporate decree away from being taken from them.

Bronnik speaks first. He tells them about Nexus-Krieg, the acquisition sweep, the repealed preservation act. He tells them about the Harken contract and the neural override weapon. He tells them everything because at this point secrets are a luxury none of them can afford.
When he finishes, the silence is the kind that has weight. Dwarven silence. The silence of people calculating loads and tolerances and deciding whether a structure can hold.
'So,' Magda says, cracking her knuckles. 'We fight or we die. Same as always.'

choices.text: 'We fight. But smart. We need a plan, not a brawl.'
'We need outside help. There's a woman named Kessa at the transit hub.'

dwf_049.title: Something New
text: The words come out before you've fully thought them through, but they feel right. Right the way metal feels right when it rings true under the hammer.

'We're dwarves, Master. We built halls inside mountains. We forged weapons that lasted longer than the civilizations that wielded them. We survived the Gene Riots, the corporate wars, the destruction of every homeland we ever had. And you know why? Because we build. It's not the forge that makes us. It's the building. The making. The shaping of raw material into something that wasn't there before.'

Bronnik looks at you for a long moment. Then he does something you've never seen him do in nine years. He laughs. Not a big laugh. Not a happy laugh. But a real one, creaking out of him like a door that hasn't been opened in decades.

'Your grandmother would have said the same thing,' he says. 'Almost word for word. Stone and fire, you're her ghost in a younger body.' He stands. 'Alright. We build something new. But first, we need a foundation. And foundations start with allies.'

choices.text: Head to the transit hub to find Kessa and her network.
Rally the other forge masters in the district first.

dwf_050.title: The Fight Option
text: 'Fight,' Bronnik repeats. He turns the word over like a piece of stock, checking it for flaws. 'Fight a megacorp with hammers and stubborn beards.'

'Not with hammers. With what we know. With the weapon specs, with the other forge masters, with anyone in this city who's tired of being squeezed.' You're on your feet now, pacing the way you do when a design is coming together in your head. 'Nexus-Krieg isn't invincible. They're a corporation. They have shareholders, competitors, regulators. They have enemies who'd love to see them bleed.'
'You're talking about politics,' Bronnik says, with the distaste of a dwarf who's spent his life believing that the only honest conversation happens between hammer and metal.
'I'm talking about war. Just the kind where the weapons are information instead of iron.'
He considers this. 'You'll need allies. People who know how the upper world works. I know a woman—'
'Kessa. At the transit hub.'
His eyebrows rise. 'How do you know about Kessa?'
'Gillis told me. Tonight, at the bar.'
'That one-armed gossip. Should have known.'

choices.text: Go find Kessa immediately.
Gather the other forge masters first. Strength in numbers.

dwf_051.title: The Deep Tunnels
text: Deeper. The tunnels get older as you descend, the concrete giving way to bare rock in places. These passages were carved during the original construction of the megacity's foundations, before the corporations decided that underground space was wasted space.

In the dark, you find others. Dwarves, mostly. Displaced forge workers, their hands still black with soot, their eyes still carrying the reflected glow of furnaces that no longer burn. They've built a camp in a junction where three tunnels meet, stringing up lights powered by a jury-rigged generator that sounds like it's running on anger.
A dwarf named Karn, tall for the race, with hands like shovels, approaches you. 'Bronnik's kid,' he says. Not a question. 'We heard about the forge. Nexus-Krieg?'

You nod. Karn spits on the ground. 'That's eight of us now. Eight masters, eight forges, all gone.' He looks at the camp, at the dwarves sitting in the dark with nothing to build. 'We've been talking. We're done running.'

choices.text: Join them. Help plan a resistance from the tunnels.
Tell them about Kessa's network. They need coordination, not isolation.

dwf_052.title: Alone in the Tunnels
text: You walk. One foot in front of the other. The bag on your back holds everything you have left: your grandmother's tools, some salvaged components, and the weight of a choice you'll carry for the rest of your life.
Behind you, if you strain, you can hear the sounds of the forge. Shouting. The crash of something heavy. Then silence. A silence that lasts too long and says too much.

You don't go back. Not because you don't want to. Because Bronnik told you to go, and a dwarf's last command to their apprentice is carved in stone. You carry his teachings forward. That's the compact. That's the tradition older than any forge or mountain hall.
The tunnels are dark, but you've always been comfortable in the dark. Dwarves were born underground. The dark is where you find the ore.

choices.text: Find the deep tunnels. Other displaced dwarves might be there.
Head for the transit hub. You need to find Kessa.

dwf_053.title: Refusing to Leave
text: 'No.' The word comes out harder than steel. 'I'm not leaving you, Master.'
Bronnik turns. His expression cycles through anger, exasperation, and something that might be pride. 'Stubborn. Stubborn as granite. Just like your—'

'My grandmother. I know.' You pick up a wrench. It's not much against tactical armor, but you're a dwarf, and dwarves have been improvising weapons since the first cave. 'We stand together or we fall together. That's the compact.'

The operatives come through the shattered gate in a wave. There are more of them this time, six, and they have a drone hovering behind them like a mechanical vulture. The fight is brutal, short, and one-sided.

You take two of them down before a taser catches you in the ribs. The world goes white, then black, then gray. You hit the floor next to Bronnik, who's already down, his hammer just out of reach. The last thing you see before consciousness leaves is a boot stepping on the anvil stamp, grinding it into the floor.

choices.text: Darkness. Then... light. Wake up.

dwf_054.title: Kessa's Mission
text: 'I need someone who can get inside a Nexus-Krieg facility,' Kessa says, pulling up schematics on her holo-projector. 'Specifically, their acquisitions office in the Market District. The legal orders they're using are based on a repealed act, but they've got a magistrate rubber-stamping everything. If I can get a copy of the communications between Nexus-Krieg and the magistrate, I can prove corruption. Bribery. Enough to get the acquisitions frozen.'

She looks at you with silver eyes that miss nothing. 'You're small. You're strong. And you know how to work with your hands in tight spaces. The ventilation system in that building was designed by dwarves, twenty years ago. The access panels are dwarf-sized.'
'You want me to break into a corporate office.'
'I want you to recover evidence of criminal activity that's destroying your community. The breaking in is just the method.'

choices.text: 'I'll do it. When do I go?'
'That's a suicide mission. I need to think about this.'

dwf_055.title: Back to the Forge
text: You pocket the encrypted comm and head back through the rain. The streets of the Lower District are darker than usual, fewer neon signs lit, more storefronts shuttered. The district is dying, shop by shop, forge by forge, and everyone can feel it even if they can't name what's killing them.
When you reach the forge, the first thing you notice is the silence. No hum of furnaces. No clang of metal. The front gate is open, cut from its hinges, lying on the ground like a fallen shield.

Inside, the forge is empty. Not empty of things, the tools are still there, the furnaces still warm, but empty of Bronnik. His cot in the back room is cold. His personal items are gone. The anvil stamp, his coat, the photograph of his mother at her forge that hung over his workbench for thirty years.
On the anvil, scratched into the iron with a chisel, two words in the old dwarven script: CARRY ON.

choices.text: Search for clues about where Bronnik went.
Honor his words. Take what you can carry and start over.

dwf_056.title: Kessa's Price
text: 'What do I want?' Kessa leans back and crosses her arms. 'Same thing everyone in the Lower District wants. To stop being ground up by the machine.' She gestures at the repair shop around her. 'I fix things. That's what I do. But I can't fix a system that's designed to break people. That takes organization. Networks. People willing to risk something.'
'I'm not an activist,' you say. 'I'm a forge worker.'

'You're a dwarf whose forge is about to be stolen. That makes you an activist whether you want to be or not.' She picks up a circuit board and begins soldering with practiced ease. 'I don't want your money. I don't want your loyalty. I want your skills. Dwarven craftsmanship is the one thing the corporations can't replicate with machines. That's why they're trying to control it.'
She sets down the soldering iron. 'So here's my price: teach me. Teach my network. Help us build the tools we need to fight back. In return, I'll help you save your forge. Or what's left of it.'

choices.text: Agree. A fair trade. Tell her about Bronnik's situation.
You don't have time for negotiations. The forge needs help now.

dwf_057.title: Scorched Earth
text: The gas line runs under the main floor. You've maintained it for years. You know exactly where the junction is, exactly how to crack the valve, exactly how long it would take for the gas to fill the space.
Bronnik sees what you're looking at and reads your mind. 'No,' he says. But it's a weak no. The kind of no that means 'convince me.'

'If they take the forge, they take your tools, your designs, everything you've built. They'll use it. They'll put corporate logos on dwarven craftsmanship and sell it back to us at a markup.' You look at him. 'Or we make sure there's nothing left to take.'

He's quiet for a long time. Then he reaches into his coat and pulls out his father's anvil stamp. He holds it up. 'This is the only thing that matters,' he says. 'The rest is iron and fire. And iron and fire are everywhere.'
He nods.

choices.text: Open the gas valve. You have minutes before it fills the space.
Wait. There has to be another way. You can't destroy the forge.

dwf_058.title: The Dwarven Stand
text: Six dwarves in a fortified forge. It's not an army, but it's more than Nexus-Krieg expected to find.

You assign positions. Kettik and Volna take the main corridor, both armed with heavy wrenches and the quiet competence of dwarves who've spent their lives handling heavy things. Therren takes the side entrance with Renna, who's already rigging something that involves wire, batteries, and a smile that concerns you. Bronnik holds the center, by his anvil, where he belongs.
And you. You move between positions, checking lines of sight, reinforcing weak points, doing what an apprentice does: the work that needs doing.
'They'll come at night,' Therren says. 'They always come at night.'
He's right. At three in the morning, the motion sensors you rigged begin to chirp.

choices.text: Sound the alarm. Everyone to positions.
Kill the lights. Let them come in blind.

dwf_059.title: Warning the Others
text: 'This isn't your fight,' you say, standing in the doorway. 'Nexus-Krieg has drones, tactical teams, legal backing. If you're caught here when they come back—'

Kettik holds up a hand. 'Let me stop you there, kid. We know what they have. We know what happens to dwarves who resist. Therren here can tell you all about it.' He looks at the old dwarf, who nods slowly. 'But we also know what happens to dwarves who don't. We end up in the scrap yards, building nothing, being nothing.'
Volna steps forward. 'A dwarf without a forge is a heart without a chest. I'd rather die standing at an anvil than live sitting in the dirt.'
Renna just grins and holds up a fistful of detonator cord. 'I brought presents.'
You look at Bronnik. He shrugs. 'Don't look at me. I stopped being able to argue with other dwarves about twenty years ago.'

choices.text: Accept their help. You're stronger together.
'If you're staying, we need a plan. Not just a last stand.'

dwf_060.title: Sending Them Away
text: You hold up a hand through the slot, palm out: danger, stay back. Kettik's eyes narrow. He follows your gaze toward the end of the alley and spots the surveillance. His expression hardens.
He signals the others, and they melt into the shadows with the practiced ease of people who've learned to move unseen in their own district. Within seconds, the alley is empty again.

You close the viewing slot and lean against the door. The watcher at the end of the alley is still there. They know the forge is occupied. They know you have visitors. Every piece of information they gather makes the next assault more efficient.
'We're being watched,' you tell Bronnik. 'The side entrance is compromised.'
He nods slowly. 'Then there's only one way out. The tunnels.'

choices.text: Take the tunnels now, before they close that exit too.
Wait until dark. Use the time to prepare.

dwf_061.title: Rest in the Dark
text: You sit against the tunnel wall and close your eyes. Not to sleep, dwarves can go days without sleep when the work demands it, but to think. To take stock of what you have and what you've lost.

What you have: your hands, your training, your grandmother's tools, the knowledge of nine years at Bronnik's side. The encrypted comm from Kessa. The stubbornness that runs through your bloodline like a vein of iron through granite.
What you've lost: everything else.

In the old stories, the heroes always lost everything before they found their purpose. You never liked those stories. They always seemed like a cruel trick, a way to make suffering into a lesson instead of just suffering.
But here you are. In the dark, with nothing, at the beginning of something you can't see the shape of yet.
You open your eyes. You stand. You keep moving.

choices.text: Head to the transit hub. Find Kessa.

dwf_062.title: Salvage Run
text: The scrap yards are a dwarf's paradise and graveyard all at once. Mountains of discarded machinery, each one containing components that, in the right hands, could be turned into something extraordinary. You pick through the heaps with practiced eyes, reading the metal the way Bronnik taught you.

You find a cracked plasma cutter that just needs a new focusing crystal. A servo motor with a bad bearing that you could fix with a paperclip and ten minutes. A crate of carbon-steel stock that some idiot threw out because the surface was oxidized, not knowing that the core was still perfect.
By the time you've filled your bag, you have enough raw material to set up a basic workbench. Not a forge. Not Bronnik's forge. But a start.

A dwarf working in the scrap heap nearby watches you sort through the salvage. 'You've got good eyes,' she says. 'Name's Renna. Used to have a forge on the east side. Nexus-Krieg.' She doesn't need to say more.

choices.text: Talk to Renna. Compare notes on Nexus-Krieg.
Head back to check on Bronnik.

dwf_063.title: Bedside Vigil
text: Patch finishes her work and wipes her hands on a cloth that was probably white once. 'The leg will heal. Six weeks, maybe eight. The implant eye is dead. I don't have the parts or the skill to fix military-grade optics.' She pauses. 'He's also got three cracked ribs and a concussion. He's too old for this.'

Bronnik, predictably, is trying to sit up. 'Too old,' he mutters. 'I'll show you too old.' But his face is gray and his hands are shaking, and for once the stubbornness isn't enough to override the damage.
You put a hand on his shoulder. 'Rest, Master. I'll handle things.'
He looks at you with his one remaining eye. 'Handle things,' he repeats. 'You're an apprentice. You're not ready.'
'You taught me for nine years. If I'm not ready now, you're a bad teacher.'
The ghost of a smile. 'Don't get clever. You're not built for it.' But he lies back. And after a while, his breathing slows and deepens and the old dwarf sleeps.

choices.text: Use the time to plan your next move with what you have.
Go explore the scrap yards while he sleeps.

dwf_064.title: Contacting Harken
text: You use the datapad to send a message to Harken's encrypted channel. Short, direct, dwarven: 'We know what you're building. We have copies of the schematics. Come talk, or they go public.'
The response comes in four minutes. Four words: 'Sector 7 bar. Midnight.'
Bronnik doesn't like it. 'Meeting a Nexus-Krieg operative in a public place is either very brave or very stupid.'
'Maybe he's not Nexus-Krieg anymore. Maybe that's why he's building this thing off the books.'
'Or maybe he wants to put a bullet in the heads of the only two people who know about his weapon.'
'Then we don't both go. One of us stays with the copies. Insurance.'
Bronnik nods slowly. 'I'll go. I know the bar. Gillis will watch my back.'
'No. I'll go. If something happens, you know where the copies are. You can carry the fight.'

choices.text: Insist on going yourself. Bronnik is too recognizable.
Let Bronnik go. He knows how to handle himself.

dwf_065.title: Waiting for the Storm
text: Waiting is the hardest part. Dwarves are built for action, for the swing of the hammer, for the problem that can be solved with hands and heat and stubborn will. Sitting in a fortified forge, watching the door, jumping at every sound, this is a different kind of torture.

The hours crawl. Bronnik sharpens tools that don't need sharpening. You check the barricades for the fifteenth time. The forge furnaces burn on, their heat the only comfort in a night that feels like it's closing in from all sides.
Dawn comes without an attack. Then noon. Then afternoon. The silence is worse than any assault.

At six in the evening, a message arrives on the datapad. Not from Harken. From someone else entirely. The text is simple: 'We know about the copies. We know about Gillis. We know about the pipe behind furnace two. You have until midnight to return the datapad and all copies, or we visit every dwarf in the district. Starting with the ones who can't fight back.'

choices.text: They're bluffing. Hold the line.
They're not bluffing. You need to warn the others.

dwf_066.title: Intel from Prisoners
text: The half-elf operative, younger than the others, talks more than he should. Fear does that to people who aren't built for it.

'The acquisition sweep finishes this week,' he says. 'Director Voss wants all independent manufacturing in the lower sectors under Nexus-Krieg control by month's end. It's part of something bigger. Something called Project Forge-Lock.' He swallows. 'That's all I know. They don't tell us the big picture.'

Bronnik leans in. 'Forge-Lock. What a name. They're not just taking the forges. They're locking down the entire independent supply chain.' He looks at you. 'If they control who builds what, they control everything that moves through the lower city. Weapons, tools, prosthetics, vehicle parts. Everything.'
'Monopoly,' you say.
'Worse. Dependence. Every dwarf, every machinist, every craftsperson in the district would have to work for them or starve.'

choices.text: This is bigger than your forge. You need allies immediately.
Project Forge-Lock needs to be exposed. Take the prisoners to Kessa.

dwf_067.title: The Plan
text: You lay it out on the workbench like a schematic. The pieces of a plan, rough-formed, needing refinement, but solid in its bones.

'Nexus-Krieg's weakness is their own process. They need legal cover, which means magistrates. They need logistics, which means supply lines. They need secrecy, because if the other corporations find out one player is trying to monopolize lower-city manufacturing, there'll be a bidding war.' You tap the bench. 'We attack on all three fronts.'
The forge masters lean in. Dwarves understand three-pronged attacks. It's how you mine a vein of ore: from three directions, converging on the prize.

'Kessa's network handles the legal angle. Evidence of corruption gets the magistrate removed. Meanwhile, we sabotage their supply lines, quietly, make their acquisition operations expensive and slow. And we leak the monopoly plan to Nexus-Krieg's competitors. Let the corporations eat each other.'
Magda grins. 'I like this dwarf. Thinks like a siege engineer.'

choices.text: Begin immediately. Send someone to Kessa. Start the sabotage.
The plan needs one more element: a public stand. Something visible.

dwf_068.title: Reaching Out
text: Bronnik nods. 'Kessa's been trying to reach the forge masters for months. Most of us were too proud or too stubborn to listen.' He looks around at the assembled dwarves. 'I suppose losing everything has a way of clearing the ears.'
You activate the encrypted comm Gillis gave you, or the one from Kessa if you have it, and send a message. The response comes quickly, as if Kessa's been waiting for this exact call.
'Forge masters organizing? Finally.' Her voice crackles through the encrypted channel. 'I have resources. People. Information. But I need a meeting point. Somewhere secure.'
Bronnik looks around his fortified forge. 'Tell her to come here. If there's one thing dwarves know how to do, it's hold a position.'
'I'm on my way,' Kessa says. 'And I'm bringing someone you need to meet. A journalist. The kind who isn't afraid of corporations.'

choices.text: Prepare the forge for the meeting. Set up a war room.
A journalist? That changes the dynamic. Discuss implications with Bronnik.

dwf_069.title: The Underground Forge
text: The displaced dwarves have done what dwarves always do: they've started building. In the wide junction where three tunnels meet, using salvaged equipment and scrounged materials, they've constructed a crude but functional forge. A converted industrial burner serves as the furnace. An engine block serves as the anvil. Tools have been cobbled together from scrap.
It's ugly. It's inefficient. It's the most beautiful thing you've seen since they took your forge.

Karn shows you around with the quiet pride of someone who's made something from nothing. 'We're producing basic tools,' he says. 'Selling them through back channels. It's not much, but it keeps the fire alive.' He taps the furnace. 'Dwarves underground, building in the dark. Like the old days. Before cities. Before corporations.'
You look at the makeshift forge and see something more than survival. You see a foundation.
'This could be more than a hiding place,' you say. 'This could be a base of operations.'

choices.text: Help them improve the forge. Apply Bronnik's techniques.
We need to connect this place to Kessa's network above.

dwf_070.title: Connecting the Networks
text: You tell them about Kessa. About the encrypted comms. About the network of displaced workers and dissidents she's been building above ground. Karn listens with his arms folded, skeptical in the way dwarves are skeptical of anything that doesn't involve metal and fire.
'A half-elf organizer,' he says flatly. 'Running a resistance out of a repair shop. And you trust her?'
'She's the only one trying to fight back in a way that might actually work. We can bang hammers all day down here, but without intelligence, without coordination, we're just making noise.'

The other dwarves exchange glances. Underground, they're safe. Connected to the surface, they're vulnerable. But safety without purpose is just a slower kind of death, and every dwarf in this tunnel knows it.
'Alright,' Karn says. 'Set up the connection. But if this goes wrong, it's your beard on the line.'

choices.text: Head to the surface to set up the link with Kessa.
Use the encrypted comm to contact Kessa from here.

dwf_071.title: Waking in Chains
text: You wake on a cold floor. Your hands are cuffed behind your back with mag-locks, the kind that tighten if you pull. Your head throbs. Your ribs feel like someone used them for an anvil.

The room is small, windowless, lit by a single strip of fluorescent that buzzes like an angry insect. Corporate holding cell. The walls are smooth polymer, featureless, designed to give you nothing to work with. A dwarf in a room with no tools is a fish out of water, and whoever put you here knows it.
Bronnik is gone. You don't know where. The absence hits harder than any blow.
A door opens. A man walks in, tall, thin, wearing a charcoal suit that probably costs more than your forge did. He sits in the chair across from you and folds his hands.
'I'm Director Voss,' he says. 'Nexus-Krieg Acquisitions. And we need to discuss your future, or more precisely, whether you have one.'

choices.text: 'Where is Bronnik? Where is my master?'
Say nothing. Give him nothing.

dwf_072.title: The Infiltration
text: Kessa's plan is clean. Too clean for comfort. You enter the Nexus-Krieg acquisitions office through a maintenance hatch in the sub-level that was designed for dwarf-sized maintenance workers. The irony isn't lost on you.

The ventilation ducts are tight but navigable. You move in silence, the way Bronnik taught you to move in the forge when working with volatile materials: weight distributed, breathing controlled, every motion deliberate.

Through a grate, you can see the acquisitions office below. Banks of holographic displays show district maps with forges marked in red. Some are crossed out. Twelve crossed out. The rest are highlighted yellow: next targets.
A terminal sits on the desk. The communications logs Kessa needs are in there. You just need five minutes and physical access.
The grate is secured with four screws. You have your grandmother's screwdriver. It takes thirty seconds.

choices.text: Drop down, access the terminal, get the data, get out.
Wait and observe. Make sure the office is truly empty.

dwf_073.title: Second Thoughts
text: You sit in Kessa's shop and turn the comm unit over in your hands. A break-in. An actual corporate espionage operation. This morning you were calibrating servo-actuators. Now you're being asked to infiltrate a megacorp's office.
'Take your time,' Kessa says, but there's urgency under the calm. 'The acquisitions sweep continues every day you hesitate. Another forge falls. Another dwarf loses their life's work.'
She's right, and you hate that she's right. You're a craftsperson, not a spy. But you're also a dwarf, and dwarves don't let their people get crushed without swinging back.
'What support do I have?'

'I can get you floor plans of the building. Entry and exit routes. A jammer that'll knock out their internal security for ninety seconds.' She meets your eyes. 'But once you're inside, you're on your own. That's the risk.'

choices.text: Ninety seconds is enough for a dwarf with good hands. Accept the mission.
There has to be another way. Ask about alternatives.

dwf_074.title: Searching for Bronnik
text: You search the forge for clues. The anvil message is all he left on the surface, but you know Bronnik. He communicates through his work, through the things he builds and the marks he leaves.

Behind the number-three furnace, the maintenance tunnel entrance is open. Fresh boot prints in the dust. He went this way, voluntarily, which means he wasn't dragged. In the tunnel, scratched into the wall at dwarf-eye-height, you find a symbol: the old forge-mark for 'deep shelter.'

He's gone underground. To the deep tunnels where the displaced dwarves have gathered. He's hurt, or hunted, or both, but he's alive and he's going to ground the way dwarves have done since the first cave.

But the forge is empty now. The gate cut open. The tools still warm. And standing here alone in the place where you learned everything you know, you feel a crack run through something inside you that you always thought was solid.

choices.text: Follow him into the tunnels. Find him.
Stay. The forge is undefended. Someone needs to hold it.

dwf_075.title: Carrying On
text: CARRY ON. Two words scratched into iron. The most Bronnik has ever said about how he feels.

You take what matters. Your grandmother's tools. The precision caliper set that took you two years to save for. A bar of high-carbon steel that Bronnik kept for special projects, the kind of steel that sings when you strike it right.
The rest you leave. The furnaces, the lathe, the plasma cutters, let Nexus-Krieg have them. Tools without the hands that know them are just metal. And the hands are yours.

You walk out of the forge for the last time. The morning rain hits your face and you don't flinch. Behind you, the forge sits silent, its furnaces dying, its anvil unattended for the first time in three generations.
Ahead of you, the city waits. Gray, vast, hungry, indifferent. But somewhere in it there are other dwarves with empty hands and burning hearts. And somewhere in it there's a fight worth having.

choices.text: Head to the transit hub. Find Kessa's network.
Go to the scrap yards. Start building something new from nothing.

dwf_076.title: The Last Fire
