text: You crack the gas valve. The hiss fills the forge like a final breath. The air thickens with the smell of unburned fuel, sweet and deadly.

Bronnik takes your arm. 'Move. Now.' You both head for the maintenance tunnel, moving as fast as his injured leg allows. Behind you, the gas fills the space where three generations of Ironhands built their legacy.

At the tunnel entrance, Bronnik turns back one last time. He looks at the forge, at the furnaces still burning, at the anvil where his father's stamp is still embedded in the iron. His lips move, shaping words in the old tongue that you can't quite hear.
Then he pulls the emergency flare from the wall bracket, strikes it against the concrete, and tosses it back into the gas-filled forge.

The explosion is enormous. It fills the tunnel behind you with fire and light, a roaring inferno that consumes everything. The walls shake. Dust rains from the ceiling. And in the heart of the blast, everything that was Bronnik's forge becomes fire and memory.
You run. You don't look back.

choices.text: Keep running. Don't stop until you're deep underground.
Head for the transit hub. You need Kessa's help now more than ever.

dwf_077.title: The Siege
text: The alarm goes up. Six dwarves snap to positions with the discipline of people who understand that preparation is the difference between survival and slaughter.

They come through the front, cutting a new hole in the repaired gate. More of them this time, eight, with two drones hovering behind them. But the forge is narrow, the barricades are solid, and dwarves fighting for their homes are worth three times their number.

The first wave hits the main barricade and stops cold. Kettik and Volna work as a team, wrenches swinging in coordinated arcs that no tactical manual prepared the operatives for. Renna's improvised traps go off in sequence, flash-bangs made from magnesium stock and industrial detonators, blinding the night-vision equipped attackers.

Bronnik holds the center like a stone pillar. His hammer rises and falls with the inevitability of a machine press. He doesn't shout. He doesn't waste breath. He just works, the way he's always worked, with precision and purpose.
The drones are the problem. They hover above the barricades, firing crowd-dispersal tasers into the defenders.

choices.text: Grab Renna's last explosive and take out the drones.
Fall back to the inner forge. Use the furnaces as cover.

dwf_078.title: Darkness as a Weapon
text: You kill the lights. All of them. Even the furnace displays. The forge plunges into absolute darkness, the kind of darkness that surface-dwellers fear in their bones but that dwarves know like an old friend.

The operatives come through with night-vision optics. Green-tinted world, shadows rendered in electronic detail. But night-vision doesn't help when someone who knows every inch of the floor is swinging a hammer from a blind spot.

The dwarves fight in the dark like they were born to it, because they were. Kettik takes down two operatives before they even know he's there. Renna, unseen, rigs the corridor behind the attackers with wire and drops a flashbang that overloads every night-vision device in the forge simultaneously.
For thirty seconds, the forge is chaos. Shouting, crashing, the clang of metal on armor. Then silence. Then Bronnik's voice, calm and rough as gravel: 'Lights.'
When the lights come up, six operatives are on the floor. The dwarves are standing. Every one of them.

choices.text: Secure the prisoners and prepare for the next wave.
This bought time, not victory. Plan the next step while you can.

dwf_079.title: Renna's Story
text: Renna is younger than you by a few years, with close-cropped red hair and hands that are already scarred from forge work. She talks fast, the way people do when they've been alone too long and suddenly have someone who understands.

'Therren's forge was the third one they took. I was asleep in the back room when they cut through the gate. Woke up to a man in tactical gear pointing a taser at my face and reading from a legal document.' She picks up a piece of scrap copper and begins bending it absently. 'Therren went with them quietly. Signed their papers. I climbed out a window.'
'Where's Therren now?'

'Somewhere. He sends messages sometimes. Short ones. He's not doing well.' She stops bending the copper. 'None of us are. A dwarf without a forge is a heart without a chest. But a heart still beats. That's the thing the corps don't understand about us. You can take everything and we'll still keep going, because stubbornness isn't a choice for dwarves. It's a biological imperative.'
She looks at your salvage pile. 'You building something?'
'Always.'

choices.text: Build a workbench together. Start making tools.
Tell her about Kessa's network. She should be part of the resistance.

dwf_080.title: Planning in the Scrap
text: You sit in Patch's converted shipping container with a piece of scrap metal and a nail, scratching out a rough plan the way Bronnik used to sketch designs on the workshop floor.

Assets: your skills, Bronnik (injured), salvaged components, the encrypted comm, and the knowledge of what Nexus-Krieg is doing. Liabilities: no forge, no money, no legal standing, and a megacorp that wants to eliminate independent manufacturing in the entire Lower District.

The equation doesn't balance. You need more on the asset side. Allies, resources, information. The scrap yards have materials but no infrastructure. Kessa's network has information but no manufacturing capability. The displaced dwarves have skills but no direction.
Put them all together and you might have something. A distributed forge, hidden, mobile, producing what the resistance needs while staying invisible to corporate surveillance.
It's a wild idea. Bronnik would call it crazy. Your grandmother would have loved it.

choices.text: Start building. Use the scrap yard as your first workshop.
Contact Kessa first. This plan needs her network to work.

dwf_081.title: Meeting at Midnight
text: The bar is nearly empty at midnight. Gillis stands behind the counter, polishing a glass with his one hand, his eyes sharp. He nods when you enter and tilts his head toward the back booth.

Harken sits in the shadows. He's not what you expected. Not a soldier, not a corporate type. He's a dwarf. Older than Bronnik, with a beard that's gone from gray to white and hands that carry the unmistakable calluses of forge work. He's drinking something amber and watching you with eyes that have seen too much.
'You're the apprentice,' he says. 'Bronnik's kid.'
'You're the man who hired us to build a neural override weapon.'

He flinches. The word weapon hits him like a physical blow. 'It wasn't supposed to be a weapon. Not originally.' He takes a drink. 'I designed it as a medical device. Neural repair system for stroke victims. Nexus-Krieg took the design and weaponized it. I went rogue to build the original version, the healing version, outside their control.'
He looks at you. 'I'm not the villain here, kid. I'm just another dwarf whose work got stolen.'

choices.text: 'Then help us. Help us fight Nexus-Krieg.'
'I don't trust you. You put my master at risk.'

dwf_082.title: Bronnik's Meeting
text: You wait in the forge while Bronnik goes to the bar. The hours crawl. Every sound is amplified by anxiety, every creak of metal or drip of water translating into threat. You sit at the workbench with a half-finished housing in your hands, but your fingers won't obey. For the first time in your life, you can't focus on the work.

At two in the morning, the side door opens. Bronnik walks in, his face unreadable. Behind him is a figure you don't expect: another dwarf, old, white-bearded, carrying himself with the careful posture of someone who's been hiding for a long time.
'This is Harken,' Bronnik says. 'He's a dwarf. He's an engineer. And the weapon he was building isn't a weapon at all.'

Harken steps forward. 'It's a neural repair device. I designed it to heal damaged brains. Nexus-Krieg stole my work and turned it into a control system.' His voice is bitter. 'I've been building the original design in secret, trying to prove what it was meant to do. But they found out, and now they want it destroyed. Along with anyone who's touched it.'
'Including us,' Bronnik says quietly.

choices.text: 'Three dwarves against a megacorp. I've heard worse odds.'
'We need more allies. Now. Tonight.'

dwf_083.title: Calling the Bluff
text: 'They're bluffing,' you say, but the words feel thin in your mouth. Bronnik looks at you with the expression of a dwarf who's been lied to by enough people to know the real thing when he sees it.
'They're not bluffing,' he says. 'They found the copies. They found Gillis. Which means they have resources we don't, and patience we can't match.' He sets down his hammer. 'We overplayed our hand.'
The midnight deadline passes in silence. At 12:01, the comm crackles. 'Time's up. Gillis says hello, by the way. He's fine. For now.'
They have your ally. They have your leverage. And now they're coming for you.

choices.text: Surrender the datapad. Gillis's life comes first.
Call Kessa. The network is your last card.

dwf_084.title: Warning the District
text: You run. Through the rain, through the neon-stained streets, hammering on forge doors and shouting warnings. The word spreads the way fire spreads through dry timber: fast, hot, and impossible to stop.

By midnight, every independent forge master in the district knows. They know about Nexus-Krieg, about the acquisition sweep, about the threat to target anyone who resists. And they know that hiding won't save them.

Some lock their doors and pray. Some pack bags and head for the tunnels. But a surprising number do something else: they open their forges, light their furnaces, and start making weapons. Not for sale. For themselves.
A dwarf named Magda finds you outside her forge. 'You started something,' she says, handing you a freshly forged wrench that's suspiciously heavy for its size. 'I hope you know how to finish it.'
'I'm a dwarf. We finish what we start.'

choices.text: Rally the forge masters. If we're fighting, we fight together.
Get Kessa involved. This needs coordination, not just courage.

dwf_085.title: Escorting the Prisoners
text: Moving three bound corporate operatives through the maintenance tunnels is not a quiet operation. They stumble, they complain, they threaten legal consequences that would be terrifying if you weren't already past the point of caring about legal anything.

The half-elf operative, the talker, keeps feeding you information as you walk. Either he's genuinely scared, or he's leading you into a trap. 'Project Forge-Lock has a timeline,' he says. 'Two weeks. After that, the acquisitions office shuts down and the legal window closes. Whatever they've taken by then, they keep. Whatever they haven't... they stop trying.'
'Two weeks,' Bronnik mutters. 'We just have to survive two weeks.'
You emerge near the transit hub and march the prisoners to Kessa's shop. She looks at the three bound operatives, then at you, then at Bronnik.
'Well,' she says. 'This is going to be interesting.'

choices.text: Help Kessa interrogate them properly. Extract everything they know.
Leave the prisoners with Kessa. You need to get back to the district.

dwf_086.title: The War Room
text: The forge transforms. Bronnik's workbench becomes a planning table. Maps of the district, drawn from memory by six forge masters who know every alley and tunnel, cover the surface. Kessa's holographic projector adds layers of data: patrol routes, corporate facility locations, communication networks.
The plan takes shape like a piece of metalwork, rough at first, then refined with each pass. Three prongs. Three simultaneous operations.

Kessa will handle the legal front: her journalist contact will break the corruption story, exposing the bribed magistrate. The forge masters will handle sabotage: carefully targeted disruptions to Nexus-Krieg's supply chains that make the acquisition sweep too expensive to continue. And you, with your infiltration skills and your knowledge of the ventilation systems, will break into the acquisitions office and steal the evidence that ties it all together.
'This is the biggest thing any of us have ever done,' Kessa says, looking around the room. 'Bigger than any forge, any contract, any piece of work. This is about what kind of city we live in.'
Bronnik picks up his hammer. 'Then let's get to work.'

choices.text: Begin the infiltration mission. The evidence is the keystone.
Start with the sabotage. Hurt their operations first.

dwf_087.title: The Public Stand
text: 'We need visibility,' you say. 'Corporations operate in the dark. They count on people being too scared or too beaten down to make noise. If we make enough noise, we become too visible to disappear.'
Magda leans forward. 'What kind of noise?'

'A demonstration. Every forge master in the district fires up their furnace at the same time. We make it visible, make it loud. We invite the feeds, the independent journalists, anyone who'll point a camera at the Lower District. Let the whole city see what's happening.'
'That's a target on every one of our backs,' Kettik says.
'We're already targets. At least this way the shot hits us in the front.' You look around the room. 'Dwarves don't hide. We've been hiding for too long already.'
The silence that follows is the dwarven kind: heavy, considered, and final. One by one, the forge masters nod.

choices.text: Organize the demonstration for tomorrow. Move fast before Nexus-Krieg can react.
Coordinate with Kessa first. Her journalist contact could amplify the message.

dwf_088.title: The Journalist
text: Kessa arrives with the journalist an hour later. Her name is Dara, human, tall and thin, with augmented eyes that record everything they see. She walks through the forge like someone cataloging evidence, which is exactly what she is.

'This is good,' she says, scanning the fortifications, the gathered dwarves, the tools of war and resistance laid out on the workbenches. 'This is a story. Little people fighting a giant. The feeds eat this up.'
'We're not little,' Bronnik growls.

'Sorry. Small-statured people fighting a giant. The point is, I can get this in front of millions of eyeballs. Nexus-Krieg's stock is already shaky after the last scandal. A story about them seizing family workshops from an ethnic community? That's a PR nightmare they'll want to make go away.'
'We don't want it to go away,' you say. 'We want it to explode.'
Dara grins. 'Now you're speaking my language.'

choices.text: Give Dara full access. Let her record everything.
Be strategic. Control the narrative. Only show what helps your cause.

dwf_089.title: Building Again
text: You build. Because that's what dwarves do. That's the answer to every question, the response to every loss, the cure for every wound: you build.

From scrap metal and salvaged components, from broken machines and discarded electronics, you construct a workbench. Then a tool rack. Then a small furnace using a repurposed industrial heater and a ventilation pipe that you angle up through a crack in the ceiling.

It's ugly. It's small. The furnace can barely reach proper forging temperature and the anvil is a truck axle stood on end. But when you strike the first piece of heated metal, when the hammer rings against the improvised anvil and sends sparks cascading through the dim light, something inside you that's been broken since they took the forge begins to mend.

Other dwarves drift over. Renna. Karn, if he's here. Others you don't know, drawn by the sound of hammering the way moths are drawn to flame. They watch you work, and you can see it in their eyes: the spark. The thing that doesn't die.
One by one, they pick up tools.

choices.text: This is just the beginning. Connect with Kessa's network and scale up.
Focus on what you can control. Build tools, build weapons, build readiness.

dwf_090.title: The Connection
text: You reach Kessa through the encrypted comm. The signal is scratchy but clear enough. You lay out everything: the displaced dwarves, the underground forge, the intelligence about Project Forge-Lock, the two-week deadline.

'Two weeks,' Kessa repeats. 'That's tight. But it's enough if we move now.' You hear her tapping on a keyboard, pulling up data. 'I have seventeen people in my network who can help. Three journalists. A former Nexus-Krieg employee who'll testify about corrupt acquisitions. And a hacker who can get us into their communications archive.'
'What do you need from us?'

'Dwarven craftsmanship. My network can gather intelligence and make noise, but we need hardware. Encryption devices, signal jammers, things that can't be traced because they're handmade by people who don't use corporate supply chains. Things that only dwarves can build.'
You look at your hands. Calloused, scarred, stained with forge-grime. These hands that learned their trade on Bronnik's anvil. These hands that can shape metal into anything.
'Tell us what you need. We'll build it.'

choices.text: Begin building equipment for the resistance immediately.

dwf_091.title: Voss's Offer
text: Voss adjusts his cuffs with manicured fingers. 'Your master is alive. For now. He's in a medical facility, being treated for his injuries. The quality of his care going forward depends entirely on this conversation.'
You pull against the mag-locks. They tighten. Voss watches with the mild interest of a man observing a lab specimen.

'You have skills,' he says. 'Exceptional skills, according to your master's own quality reports, which we now have access to. Nexus-Krieg values craftsmanship. We'd like to offer you a position in our precision manufacturing division. Full salary, housing in the mid-district, medical coverage for you and your master.'
'And in exchange?'
'You build what we tell you to build. You don't ask questions. And you forget about the Harken project entirely.'
A job. A real job. Medical care for Bronnik. A life that doesn't involve running or fighting or sleeping in tunnels. All you have to do is everything your grandmother would have killed you for.

choices.text: Accept. Bronnik needs medical care. Survival comes first.
Refuse. 'I'd rather forge with my teeth than build for you.'

dwf_092.title: Dwarven Silence
text: You say nothing. Voss waits. A minute passes. Then two. The fluorescent light buzzes. The silence stretches until it becomes its own kind of weapon.

Voss is not accustomed to silence. Corporate men live in a world of constant negotiation, of words as currency, of every pause filled with leverage and counter-leverage. Your silence unnerves him. You can see it in the way his fingers tighten on each other.
'Your master talked,' he says finally. 'Told us everything about the Harken project. The schematics, the deployment plans, all of it.'
You know he's lying. Bronnik would swallow his own tongue before giving information to a corporate director. But Voss doesn't know you know, and that's its own kind of advantage.
You continue to say nothing. Let him fill the silence. Let him make mistakes.

'Fine,' Voss says, standing. 'You can sit here and be stubborn. But stubbornness doesn't feed you, doesn't heal your master, and doesn't get you out of this room.' He walks to the door. 'I'll give you twenty-four hours to reconsider.'
The door closes. You're alone. And in the silence, you begin to plan.

choices.text: Study the room. There's always a way out. Always.

dwf_093.title: The Data Heist
text: You drop through the grate like a stone through water. The office floor is cold under your boots. The terminal hums, unlocked, its screen casting blue light across the empty room.

Your fingers move across the interface with the same precision you bring to the forge. You're not a hacker, but you don't need to be. The files are right there, labeled with the bureaucratic clarity of an organization that never expected a dwarf to drop through their ceiling.
ACQUISITION ORDERS - SECTOR 7. MAGISTRATE CORRESPONDENCE - CLASSIFIED. PROJECT FORGE-LOCK - EXECUTIVE SUMMARY. You copy everything onto a data chip, your grandmother's hands steady in yours.
Sixty seconds gone. Thirty left on Kessa's jammer.

You grab the chip and jump for the grate. Your fingers catch the edge. You pull yourself up with upper-body strength that comes from nine years of swinging hammers. The screws go back in. The grate seals.
Twenty seconds later, the security system comes back online. By then, you're three corridors away and moving fast.

choices.text: Get the data to Kessa. This is the evidence that ends Forge-Lock.

dwf_094.title: Patience Above
text: You wait. Watching through the grate with the patience of a dwarf who's spent nine years learning that rushing a weld means redoing a weld.

Good thing you waited. Three minutes later, a door opens and a cleaning drone enters the office. It sweeps the floor, empties a waste bin, and runs a UV sanitization pass over the desk surfaces. Its sensors would have detected you instantly.
The drone finishes and leaves. You count to sixty. Then another sixty. Then you move.

The drop is clean, the data extraction faster now that you've watched the terminal and know exactly which folders to target. You're back in the vent in forty-five seconds, the data chip warm in your pocket, your heart hammering against your ribs.
Sometimes the dwarven virtue isn't strength or stubbornness. Sometimes it's just knowing when to wait.

choices.text: Get out clean. Deliver the data to Kessa.

dwf_095.title: Holding the Empty Forge
text: You stay. Someone has to. The forge stands empty, its gate cut open, its master gone, and if no one holds it then Nexus-Krieg walks in and takes it without a fight. And a dwarf's forge, taken without a fight, is a shame that follows your bloodline for generations.

You repair the gate as best you can. You fire up the furnaces. You pick up Bronnik's hammer, the one that's too heavy for you but that you grip anyway because the weight of it means something. And you stand at the anvil, alone, in a forge built for a master and his apprentice, and you work.
Not because there are orders to fill. Not because anyone is watching. Because the sound of a hammer on an anvil is a dwarf's way of saying I'm still here. I haven't broken. Come and try.
The hours pass. Night falls. And somewhere in the dark city above and below, the wheels of something larger than you can see continue to turn.

choices.text: Keep working. Let the hammer speak for you.

dwf_096.title: Taking the Drones
text: Renna tosses you her last improvised explosive: a magnesium charge packed into a ball bearing canister. 'Pull the pin, throw high, don't miss,' she yells over the noise of combat.

You don't miss. You're a dwarf. You've been throwing hammers since before you could walk. The charge detonates between the two drones in a flash of white-hot magnesium that turns the air itself into fire. Both drones drop like stones, their optics melted, their rotors fused.
The operatives, now without air support and facing six very angry dwarves in a fortified position, begin to withdraw. They drag their wounded through the gate and disappear into the rainy night.
The forge holds. The dwarves hold. But everyone knows this was just the first real assault. The next one will be bigger.
Bronnik wipes blood from his hammer. 'That's the third gate they've cut through. I'm running out of steel to repair it.'
'Then we don't repair it,' you say. 'We improve it.'

choices.text: Salvage the drone parts. A dwarf can make anything from anything.
This can't go on. It's time for a different approach.

dwf_097.title: The Inner Forge
text: You fall back to the inner forge, the heart of the workshop where the main furnaces burn. The heat here is intense, a wall of shimmering air that the operatives have to push through. In their tactical gear, they're cooking. In your forge-hardened skin, you're home.

The furnaces become your allies. You know their output, their reach, the way heat pools and flows through the space. You position the defenders where the heat is worst for the attackers and best for you.

The fight becomes a war of attrition. They have numbers. You have terrain, heat, and the kind of stubbornness that's encoded in dwarven DNA. Every meter they take costs them, and the cost keeps going up.

But so does yours. Kettik takes a taser hit and goes down. Volna drags him behind a furnace, her face set like stone. Therren is bleeding from a cut above his eye. Even Bronnik is slowing, his swings losing the machine-precise rhythm that makes him deadly.
You need to end this. Now.

choices.text: Open the furnace doors. Flood the space with heat and light.
Retreat to the tunnels. Save the people, even if you lose the forge.

dwf_097b.title: The Alliance Forms
text: Pieces fall into place like components in a mechanism. Harken's knowledge of Nexus-Krieg's operations. Kessa's network of dissidents and informants. The forge masters' craftsmanship and determination. Your own hands, trained by the best smith in the Lower District.

In Gillis's back room, over drinks that could strip paint, a plan takes shape. Not a perfect plan. Not even a good plan by corporate standards. But a dwarven plan, which means it's built on foundations of stubbornness, ingenuity, and the absolute refusal to accept that the situation is hopeless.
'Two weeks,' Kessa says. 'That's the Forge-Lock deadline. If we can survive two weeks and make enough noise, the window closes and the acquisitions stop.'
'Two weeks,' Bronnik says, if he's there. 'I've done harder forging jobs.'
Around the table, dwarves and allies nod. It's not hope, exactly. It's something more dwarven than hope. It's work to be done.

choices.text: Begin the resistance. Two weeks of fighting starts now.

dwf_097c.title: Distrust
text: Harken takes the accusation like a hammer blow. His shoulders sag. His old hands, forge-scarred and trembling, wrap around his glass.

'You're right,' he says. 'I did. I brought danger to Bronnik's door because I was desperate and selfish and scared.' He meets your eyes. 'I'm still all of those things. But I'm also a dwarf who watched his life's work get turned into a weapon. And I'll spend whatever's left of my life trying to undo that.'

You study him. Read him the way Bronnik taught you to read metal: looking for the grain, the flaws, the hidden strengths. What you see is an old dwarf at the end of his road, carrying a weight that would crush most people.
'Prove it,' you say. 'Help us fight Nexus-Krieg. Not with weapons. With the truth about what they stole from you.'
He nods. 'I can do that. I have documentation. Everything they took, everything they changed. Enough to bring a case that would make their legal department weep.'

choices.text: Accept his help. Use his evidence alongside Kessa's network.

dwf_097d.title: Surrender
text: You hand over the datapad. It feels like handing over a piece of your skeleton. The operative who takes it doesn't even look at it, just seals it in an evidence bag with practiced indifference.

Gillis is released the next morning, missing two teeth and his remaining arm in a sling. He doesn't blame you. He doesn't blame anyone. He just goes back to his bar and pours drinks with a hand that shakes now when it didn't before.

The forge goes quiet. Bronnik sits at his anvil and doesn't move for hours. The fire in his eyes, the one that's burned since he was six years old and his mother put a hammer in his hand, flickers. It doesn't go out. But it's smaller than you've ever seen it.

You've lost the leverage. You've lost the fight. But you haven't lost yourself. And somewhere in the ruins of everything you planned, you find something unexpected: clarity. The kind that only comes when you've hit bottom and the only direction left is up.
It's time to rebuild. Not the forge. Not the plan. Yourself.

choices.text: Start over. Find Kessa. There's more than one way to fight.

dwf_097e.title: The Road Ahead
text: The rain hasn't stopped. It never stops in the Lower District, as if the sky itself is trying to wash the city clean and failing, day after day, year after year.

You stand in the street outside what remains of your life, whatever that looks like now, and you take stock. You have your hands. You have your training. You have the stubbornness of a bloodline that has survived every catastrophe the world has thrown at it. And for the first time in your life, you have a purpose that extends beyond the next forging job.

The megacity stretches around you in every direction, a vertical maze of neon and concrete and human misery, a machine designed to grind people into fuel. But machines have parts. And parts can be removed, replaced, or sabotaged by someone who understands how they work.
You're a dwarf. You understand how everything works.

Somewhere in this city, there's a fight waiting for you. Not just for your forge, not just for your people, but for everyone who's been told they're too small, too poor, too insignificant to matter. You've been building things your whole life. Time to build something that actually changes the world.
You pull your coat tight against the rain and start walking.

choices.text: The Forge Keeper — You saved the forge or built a new one. Your craft endures.
The Resistance Fighter — You joined the fight against Nexus-Krieg. Hammers and data.
The Exile — You lost everything but carried Bronnik's legacy into the unknown.
The Infiltrator — You discovered your talent for shadow work. A new kind of craft.

dwf_097f.title: The Corporate Dwarf
text: The word yes tastes like ash in your mouth. But Bronnik's broken body in a medical facility somewhere in this building makes the decision for you. Survival first. Revenge later.

Voss smiles. It's the smile of a man who's won, and who's comfortable winning. 'Excellent decision. You'll be assigned to our precision manufacturing division. Level twelve. Your security clearance will be limited, but your workshop will be... adequate.'

Adequate. He has no idea what that word means to a dwarf. But you nod, and you sign, and you let them remove the mag-locks and escort you to a room that's cleaner than anything you've ever lived in. It has a window. Through it, you can see the Lower District far below, a maze of darkness and neon that holds everything you love.
You press your hand against the glass. On the other side, the city doesn't care. It never has. But you do. And a dwarf who cares about something is a dwarf who will eventually act on it.
They think they've bought you. They don't understand: you can't buy a dwarf. You can only borrow them.

choices.text: Bide your time. Learn their weaknesses from the inside.

dwf_097g.title: Dwarven Refusal
text: 'I'd rather forge with my teeth than build for the people who stole from my master.'
Voss's expression doesn't change. He's heard refusals before. They're just a data point in his cost-benefit analysis. 'Unfortunate. Your master's medical care will be... adjusted accordingly.'
The threat lands. It lands hard. But you've been hit harder. Bronnik's hammer on a bad day hits harder than anything this man in his expensive suit can throw.
'Bronnik would rather die free than live as your tool,' you say. 'And so would I.'

Voss stands. 'Then you'll be released. Into the Lower District, with nothing. No forge, no master, no resources. See how long your principles keep you warm.' He walks to the door. 'We'll be watching. When you change your mind, and you will, the offer stays open. But the terms get worse.'
The door opens. Two guards escort you to a service exit that deposits you in a rain-slicked alley in the Market District. You're free. You have nothing. You have everything.

choices.text: Find your way back to the Lower District. The fight continues.

dwf_097h.title: The Escape
text: The room is polymer. Smooth, featureless, designed to give you nothing. But you're a dwarf, and you've spent your life looking at materials and seeing what they can become.

The mag-lock cuffs are the weak point. Not the locks themselves, those are military grade, but the power supply. A small battery pack on the left cuff, no bigger than a coin. If you can short it, the electromagnets release.
You work your hands behind your back, feeling with fingers that can detect a half-micron tolerance by touch. The battery housing has a seam. Your thumbnail, hardened by years of forge work, finds it.
It takes twenty minutes of patient, silent work. The kind of work Bronnik would approve of: no wasted motion, no rushing, just steady pressure applied to the right point until the thing gives way.
The cuff pops. Your hands are free.

The door has a manual override, standard safety requirement. You find it, trigger it, and step into a corridor that stretches in both directions. Somewhere in this building, Bronnik is being held. Somewhere in this building, Director Voss is making plans.
But right now, the priority is out.

choices.text: Find Bronnik first. You won't leave without him.
Get out, regroup, come back with help.

dwf_097i.title: Finding Bronnik
text: The corporate building's corridors are clean and white and identical. But you navigate them the way you navigate tunnels: by feel, by instinct, by the way sound travels through enclosed spaces. A medical ward has a particular hum, the sound of monitoring equipment and filtered air.

You find it on the third floor. Four rooms, two occupied. In the second one, behind a locked door that your forge-hardened fingers defeat in thirty seconds, Bronnik lies on a hospital bed. His leg is in a brace. His implant eye socket is bandaged. But he's alive, and when he sees you, his one good eye goes wide.
'You stubborn, reckless, granite-headed fool,' he says. Which is Bronnik for I'm glad to see you.
'Can you walk?'
'I can't not walk. Get me out of this bed.'

You help him up. He's heavy, damaged, running on dwarven stubbornness and not much else. Together, you move through the corridors toward a service exit that your forge-trained instincts tell you must exist, because every building needs maintenance access, and maintenance access is always built for the workers, not the executives.
You emerge into rain and freedom. The Lower District glows below you like a dying fire.

choices.text: Get Bronnik to safety. Then plan the next move.

dwf_098.title: Ending: The Forge Keeper
text: You saved what mattered. Whether it's Bronnik's original forge, battered and scarred but still standing, or a new forge built from scrap and determination in the tunnels beneath the city, you kept the fire alive. Dwarves have lost kingdoms, lost mountains, lost entire civilizations, but they've never lost the ability to shape metal with their bare hands.

The forge you tend now is more than a workshop. It's a statement. A refusal. A raised fist made of iron and fire, telling the megacorps and their acquisition orders and their legal documents that some things can't be bought because they aren't for sale.

Bronnik's teachings live in your hands. Your grandmother's stubbornness lives in your spine. And the fire, the fire that's been burning in dwarven forges since before humans learned to stack stones, burns in you.

But fires need fuel. And the city outside your forge is changing, shifting, building toward something that you can feel in the vibrations of the ground beneath your feet. Something big is coming. Something that will need a dwarf with steady hands and an unbreakable will.
You pick up your hammer. You always pick up your hammer. And you wait for what comes next.

choices.text: Begin the prologue.

dwf_098b.title: Ending: The Resistance Fighter
text: You joined something bigger than yourself. Kessa's network, the forge masters' council, the alliance of displaced workers and dissidents who refuse to accept that the city belongs to the corporations. You brought dwarven craftsmanship to the resistance and discovered that a hammer can build more than tools. It can build movements.

The fight against Nexus-Krieg isn't over. It may never be over. But you've proven something that the corporate directors in their tower offices never understood: that the people they step on are not stepping stones. They're foundations. And foundations, when they shift, bring down everything built on top of them.

Your forge skills now serve a larger purpose. Encryption devices built by hand, untraceable. Signal jammers that no scanner can detect because they're made of components no machine assembled. Weapons, yes, when necessary, but also tools of communication, of coordination, of defiance.
The megacity hasn't changed. Not yet. But underneath its surface, in the tunnels and workshops and hidden forges where dwarves do what dwarves have always done, the ground is shifting.
And you're one of the ones pushing.

choices.text: Begin the prologue.

dwf_099.title: Ending: The Exile
text: You lost the forge. You may have lost Bronnik. You lost everything that defined you as a dwarf and a craftsperson. And in the empty space where all of that used to be, you found something unexpected: freedom.

Not the comfortable kind. Not the kind they sell in advertisements with smiling people and clean air. The raw kind. The dangerous kind. The freedom of having nothing left to lose and nowhere left to fall.

You carry Bronnik's teachings in your hands. Your grandmother's tools in your bag. And in your chest, a fire that burns hotter for having been nearly extinguished. The old dwarven stories talk about the Wandering Forge, a mythical place where displaced craftspeople gather to rebuild what was lost. You used to think it was a fairy tale.
Now you think it might be a destination.

The city stretches ahead of you, vast and indifferent. But somewhere in its depths, in tunnels you haven't found yet and districts you haven't walked, there are other dwarves with empty hands and full hearts. And where dwarves gather, forges follow.
You walk into the dark with your tools on your back and your legacy in your blood.

choices.text: Begin the prologue.

dwf_099b.title: Ending: The Infiltrator
text: You discovered something about yourself in those ventilation ducts and corporate corridors: you're good at this. Not just the forge work, which will always be your foundation, but the shadow work. The watching, the waiting, the moving through spaces where you're not supposed to be and leaving no trace.

Dwarves are builders. But the best builders understand structures from the inside, know where the joints are weakest, where the load-bearing walls hide, where one precise blow can bring down an entire floor. You've taken that understanding and applied it to a different kind of structure: the corporate machine.

Kessa's network has a new asset. A dwarf who can go where others can't, build what others can't, and find information that others would die for. Your grandmother's tools still sit in your bag, but beside them now are lock picks, data slicers, and a jammer of your own design that's small enough to hide in your beard.
The megacity is full of locked doors. And you've discovered that behind every locked door is a secret that someone is willing to pay for, or fight for, or change the world with.
You've become something new. Something the old dwarven stories never imagined. A dwarf of the shadows, forged in loss and tempered by survival.

choices.text: Begin the prologue.

dwf_100.title: Legacy of Iron
text: Before you leave this origin behind, you pause. You look at your hands, these hands that Bronnik shaped as surely as he shaped metal. They're scarred, calloused, stained with the residue of a thousand forgings. They're the most valuable things you own.

Whatever comes next, whatever the prologue brings, whatever the city throws at you, you face it as a dwarf. Not just any dwarf. An Ironhand apprentice. The last student of a master whose forge burned for three generations. A craftsperson who carries the fire inside when the forge outside is cold.
The rain falls. The city hums. And somewhere in the sound of it, if you listen with a dwarf's ears, you can hear the ring of a hammer on an anvil, steady and sure and endless.
Your story is just beginning.

choices.text: Continue to the prologue.
